name: Sarvajnatman
dates_notes: Disciple-tradition links to Sureśvara; modern dating varies between the 10th and 11th centuries.
core_thesis: Sarvajñātman, claiming descent from Sureśvara through Devasvāmin, builds the proto-*Vivaraṇa* Advaita that Prakāśātman will later systematise. The locus of *avidyā* is Brahman — pure *cit* — and not the *jīva*. The *jīva* is itself *cidābhāsa*, a reflection (*pratibimba*) of Brahman cast in beginningless ignorance. Sarvajñātman gives this *bimba-pratibimba-vāda* its first systematic Advaitic statement, with the standard analogy: one face appears reflected in many mirrors without being multiplied, and so the Brahman-*bimba* appears as plural *jīva-pratibimbas* without compromising non-duality. From this root three further commitments follow. The Mahāvākya is the unique *pramāṇa* productive of *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti*. *Karma* and *upāsanā* remove *vikṣepa* and *mala* but never produce *jñāna* directly; any strong *samuccaya* doctrine is therefore rejected. Liberation is the *nivṛtti* of avidyā by knowledge alone. Prakāśātman takes these positions over and refines them in the *Vivaraṇa*; they form the structural backbone of the post-Padmapāda Advaita line.

engaged_works.title: Samkshepa-Shariraka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-systematization of Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Four-*adhyāya* metrical *vārttika* in over 2,200 *anuṣṭubh* verses, compressing and argumentatively reconstructing Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* and *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* establish the broad principles of the Sureśvara line; the *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* welds them into a *Brahma-sūtra*-shaped system. Four positions are established. First, Brahman is the locus of *avidyā*, explicitly against what will become the *Bhāmatī* alternative. Second, the *jīva* is *cidābhāsa*, analysed by *bimba-pratibimba*. Third, the Mahāvākya is the unique productive *pramāṇa* of *akhaṇḍa-jñāna*. Fourth, *karma* has no role in producing knowledge but retains a purificatory function. The work generates a substantial commentarial tradition — Madhusūdana's *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka-Sāra-Saṃgraha*, Rāmatīrtha's *Anvayārtha-Prakāśikā*, Nṛsiṃhāśrama's *Tattvabodhinī* — and supplies the textual base with which later Advaita reckons.

engaged_works.title: Pancha-Prakriya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; ascription is generally accepted in tradition with limited modern critical engagement.
engaged_works.summary: Short pedagogical *prakaraṇa* organising the central themes of Sarvajñātman's Advaita under five *prakriyās*, the heuristic procedures by which the student is led from ordinary self-misidentification to recognition of the *akhaṇḍa* identity. The five-fold rubric typically tracks: the *avasthā-traya-viveka* analysing waking, dream, and deep sleep to isolate the witness; the *pañca-kośa-viveka* of the *Taittirīya*; the analytic location of *avidyā* in Brahman qua *cit*; the *bimba-pratibimba* exposition of the *jīva*; and the function of *Mahāvākya-vicāra* in producing the *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti*. The work matters as a compressed handbook of Sarvajñātman-line *prakriyā* whose categories recur in later Vivaraṇa pedagogy. Modern critical engagement remains limited; the ascription is traditional rather than colophon-secured.
